figures.caption: FIG. 1. Jaynes–Cummings control. (a) Energy level di- agram showing JC (cyan) and ancilla qubit (orange) interac- tions in a rotating frame where the qubit and oscillator have a JC detuning. The ancilla qubit is encoded in the transmon’s |g⟩and |f⟩states. The JC interaction is implemented us- ing a sideband drive detuned by δ from the |f, d −1⟩↔|g, d⟩ transition, where d−1 is a chosen photon-number cutoff. Cor- responding transitions for other photon numbers are further detuned by dispersive shifts. The pulse duration is chosen to implement a 2π rotation on the cutoff transition, closing the dynamics to the subspace within the green box. Ancilla qubit relaxation errors send the state to |e⟩, which is outside of the computational subspace, allowing for their detection. (b) Circuit representation of the JC protocol. An arbitrary unitary can be approximated by a sequence of JC and ancilla qubit rotations.

FIG. 2. Scaling analysis. (a) Gate infidelity for a shift gate as a function of circuit depth, for different qudit dimen- sions. The solid and dotted lines indicate optimization with and without JC detuning, respectively. (b) Circuit depth required to reach a target fidelity as a function of qudit di- mension, fit to a quadratic function. (c) Quadratic prefactor of the shift gate circuit depth as a function of target infidelity, showing logarithmic scaling and a reduction with detuning op- timization. (d) For Haar random unitaries, the circuit depth required to reach a target fidelity as a function of qudit dimen- sion displays a quadratic scaling. (e) Circuit depth required to reach > 99% fidelity as a function of |χf/gJC|, for different qudit dimensions. Optimizing with a JC detuning leads to a significant improvement in circuit depth.

FIG. 3. Qutrit shift gate. (a) Illustration of a qutrit shift gate in an oscillator. Experimentally reconstructed Wigner functions are shown for six input states (top row) and the corresponding output states after the shift gate (bottom row). (b) Sequence used for joint qubit-oscillator tomography. The oscillator state is measured using Wigner tomography after postselecting on four qubit basis states. (c) Angles of the ancilla qubit and JC rotations, and JC detunings, for a qutrit shift gate. (d) Pulse sequence corresponding to the rotation angles in (c). (e) Populations P (|g, n⟩) and P (|f, n⟩) as a function of circuit layer, measured using joint qubit–oscillator tomography for a qutrit shift gate initialized in |g0⟩, |g1⟩, and |g2⟩. The markers show the experimentally measured populations and the lines display the simulated decoherence-free evolution.

FIG. 4. Process tomography of universal qutrit gates. Absolute value of the process matrix for a qutrit (a) shift gate X, (b) X2 gate, (c) Hadamard gate, (d) phase gate S, (e) T gate, and (f) π/2 Givens rotation in the |0⟩-|1⟩ subspace. The solid black lines indicate the ideal gate. The full process matrices are shown in Figs. S9 and S10 of the SI. (g) Process fidelities of the universal qutrit gates after post-selection are shown as solid blue markers. Grey markers indicate results of Lindblad master-equation simulations. The blue hollow markers show the process fidelity obtained when the implemented gate is compared to the target gate followed by a qudit phase gate, R(φ)Utarget, where R(φ) = ∑ j e ijφ |j⟩ ⟨j|. Error bars on the fidelities are obtained by extracting errors in the density matrix reconstruction from Wigner tomography following Ref. [15], and propagating them through the process matrix reconstruction. (h) Sequence used for qutrit process tomography.

FIG. S1. Calibration of sideband dynamical phase. (a) Ramsey experiment with increasing layers of 2π sideband ro- tations. The offset phase between sequential layers can be used to extract the dynamical phase. (b) Dynamical phase accumulation as a function of number of 2π sideband layers for the |f2⟩-|g3⟩transition. The slope of the fit is the dynam- ical phase of a 2π rotation (c) (top) Time of a 2π rotation as a function of detuning for the |f0⟩-|g1⟩transition. (Below) The measured dynamical phase as a function of sideband de- tuning. The solid line is the theoretically predicted dynamical phase from Eq. S9 based on independently calibrated Hamil- tonian parameters.

FIG. S2. Characterization of qubit theta calibration. (a) Measured versus expected qubit theta rotation angle. The grey line is the ideal calibration. The inset shows the prob- ability of |e⟩as a function of number of θ rotations. The period of a sinusoidal fit can be used to extract the value of θ. (b) Difference between the measured and expected theta angle. The error begins to increase below 10 degrees, indi- cating where the DAC becomes nonlinear. We constrain the optimizer to only optimize over theta angles where the angle is linear.

FIG. S3. Sideband Stark shift stabilization. (a) Side- band spectroscopy protocol. (b) Protocol for stabilizing the sideband resonance frequency. (c) Measured fluctuations of the |f⟩-state Stark shift with and without stabilization. (d) Fidelity of the πf0g1 pulse, extracted from the decay of a side- band pulse train with Stark-shift stabilization.

FIG. S4. Multistart gradient descent convergence. Fidelity as a function of optimization step for 1000 random initial- izations, for a qutrit Hadamard gate optimized using (a) 4 circuit layers and (b) 6 layers. For a circuit depth of 4, the fidelity reaches 85.10%; a circuit with 6 layers reaches 99.9%.

FIG. S5. Robustness of JC control protocol to Hamil- tonian parameters. Change in process fidelity as a func- tion of Hamiltonian parameter and pulse miscalibration, for a shift gate optimized for 99.9% fidelity. The parameters being swept are the dispersive shifts for |e⟩(∆χe) and |f⟩(∆χf), the driven Stark shifts for the transmon (∆fss) and oscilla- tor (∆f osc ss ), the detunings in the |g⟩-|e⟩(∆fge) and sideband drives (∆fsb), and the pulse lengths for the |g⟩-|e⟩(∆τge) and sideband (∆τsb) drives. The grey line indicates a 1% drop in fidelity.

FIG. S6. Qutrit Clifford gates. (a) Histograms of the number of circuit layers to implement all 216 qutrit Clifford gates with 99% fidelity, optimized with (blue) and without (orange) JC detuning. Simulated gate fidelities using device parameters, including decoherence, with (b) and without (c) post-selection. (d) Heatmap of the average circuit depth to implement a Clifford gate generated by products of H and S, with (left) and without (right) JC detuning optimization.

FIG. S7. Ququart and ququint shift gates. Experi- mentally reconstructed Wigner functions for input Fock-basis states (top row) and the corresponding output states after the shift gate (bottom row) for (a) the ququart X gate and (b) the ququint X gate. (c) The blue markers show the post- selected state fidelity averaged over the set of input Fock states. For reference, the orange marker shows the gate fi- delity inferred from the qutrit shift gate process matrix.

TABLE S1. State preparation for qudit process tomography. State preparation sequences used for preparing input states used for qudit process tomography. In the qutrit case, the states prepared using the JC control protocol were implemented in four circuit layers.

FIG. S8. Post-selection fraction. (a) Readout histograms after preparing the transmon in the |g⟩, |e⟩, and |f⟩states. (b) Three-state transmon readout confusion matrix. (c) Fraction of gate shots in |e⟩and |f⟩discarded by post- selection for all qutrit gates shown in Fig. 4 of the main text.
TABLE S2. Lindblad master equation parameters. Jump operators used in Lindblad master equation simulations and their respective timescales

FIG. S9. Process tomography of universal qutrit gates. Real and imaginary parts of the process matrices for the qutrit (a) X gate, (b) X2 gate, (c) Hadamard gate, (d) phase S gate, (e) T gate, and (f) π/2 Givens rotation in the |0⟩-|1⟩subspace. The solid, black lines indicate the ideal gate.

FIG. S10. Process tomography of universal qutrit gates. Real and imaginary parts of the process matrices for a (a) π/2 Givens rotation in the |0⟩-|2⟩subspace and (b) a π/2 Givens rotation in the |1⟩-|2⟩. The solid, black lines indicate the ideal gate.

FIG. S11. Error budget of qutrit gates. (a) Error budget for qutrit gates from Lindblad master-equation simulations. Light colors show the error budget without post-selecting on the transmon being in |g⟩(right bar), while dark colors show the corresponding error budget with post-selection (left bar). Without post-selection, the dominant source of infidelity is transmon decay followed by transmon dephasing. Post-selection reduces all infidelities and notably removes a significant fraction of transmon decay errors. After post-selection, transmon dephasing becomes the dominant source of error. (b) Breakdown of the coherent errors in (a), including optimizer error, Hamiltonian terms not included in the optimization, and pulse-timing errors from rounding pulse lengths to integer multiples of the fabric clock period of the FPGA used in our experiments. The omitted Hamiltonian terms include |g⟩-|e⟩and |e⟩-|f⟩Stark shifts from the transmon drives, and oscillator Stark shifts from the sideband drive.

Fig. S11(a) shows the error budget obtained from Lindblad master-equation simulations, compared with the measured process fidelities from process tomogra- phy. The error budget is constructed by running master- equation simulations with individual decoherence chan-
